headline: From the Brink: Australia's Numbat Makes a Stunning Comeback
meta_description: After 40 years of conservation effort, Australia's numbat has recovered from endangered status. Numbers have grown from 300 to 2,000-3,000 individuals.
summary_short: Once nearly extinct with only 300 left, Western Australia's striped numbat is thriving again thanks to four decades of dedicated conservation work.
body:
By the late 1970s, the numbat—a striped, reddish-brown marsupial that eats ants and termites—was nearly gone. Just 300 individuals remained in the wild. Today, thanks to 40 years of sustained effort, the species has recovered so dramatically that the IUCN has downlisted it from Endangered to Near Threatened.

The numbers tell the story: around 2,000 to 3,000 numbats now live across southern Australia, with one original Western Australian population growing to approximately 2,000. What made this recovery possible was unglamorous, persistent work—scientists, wildlife officials, zoo staff, and volunteers baited and removed introduced predators like red foxes and feral cats that had devastated the population. Teams also built fenced sanctuaries and established five new populations using wild and zoo-bred numbats, some now persisting for more than 30 years.

Yet success remains fragile. Tony Friend, a research associate with Western Australia's department of biodiversity, conservation and attractions, offers both celebration and caution: the species' numbers, while growing, remain "very low for an entire species." Continued predator control is essential. One drought, disease outbreak, or lapse in management could reverse decades of progress.

The numbat's recovery proves that extinction isn't inevitable—but it demands unwavering commitment.
